title: What Do Market Gardeners Grow? Crops, Strategy, and Why Herbs Come First
metaDescription: Market gardeners grow high-value crops that pay off fast on small plots — herbs, salad mix, radishes, microgreens, and more. Scotty breaks down what sells, what doesn't, and how to start smart.

content.opening: Y'all, I've been selling at farmers markets in the Houston area for about five years now. Bay Area Farmers Market, Bay Brook, Urban Harvest — I've stood behind a table in the Texas heat and watched people walk past booths, stop, look, buy, and come back the next week. And the one thing I've learned from all that time is this: what you grow matters a whole lot less than whether you understand why you're growing it. Market gardening is not just gardening. It's growing with intention. It's growing for your community. And I'm about to share everything I know about what grows, what sells, and where to start.
content.directAnswer: Market gardeners grow intensive, high-value crops on small plots — typically under two acres — to sell directly to consumers at farmers markets, through CSA shares, or to local restaurants. The most common crops include fresh herbs, salad mix, microgreens, radishes, tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers, and specialty greens. The most profitable crops are the ones with fast turn times, low input costs, and strong repeat demand from a loyal customer base. Herbs check all three boxes. That's where I tell every new grower to start.

sections.heading: The Most Profitable Market Garden Crops
sections.body:
Let me give y'all the data before I give you the Scotty opinions. Because the research backs up what the best market gardeners already know intuitively.

According to ATTRA — the National Sustainable Agriculture Information Service, which is free for anyone to use and one of the best resources in American farming — market gardening is defined as the intensive production of high-value crops from just a few acres, specifically designed to increase farmer income. That's the whole model. Small space. High value. Direct market.

Here's what actually moves money at the table, backed by real numbers:

**Fresh herbs** can earn over $30,000 per acre per year. Basil alone is one of the best sellers at any farmers market in the country, and chefs need bulk supply of fresh basil on a consistent, weekly basis. You're not just growing food — you're growing a relationship with buyers who come back every single week.

**Salad greens and baby mix** generate around $20 per pound and produce a new harvest every 21 days under intensive cut-and-come-again management. That's a fast cycle. When one bed is ready to harvest, another one is already coming up behind it.

**Microgreens** sit at the top of the per-square-foot revenue chart. Penn State Extension's business planning resources document microgreen operations earning up to $50 per square foot per harvest cycle. At those margins, a 100-square-foot operation is not a hobby. It's a business.

**Radishes** — and I'll talk a lot more about these in a minute — mature in as little as 25 days and sell for $3 to $4 per bunch. For a market gardener running on tight bed space, a 25-day crop that brings in $3 to $4 a bundle is a cash flow machine.

**Culinary herb bundles** — basil, thyme, mint — fetch $3 to $4 per bunch with weekly harvests on the same plants for most of the season.

The USDA counted 1.88 million U.S. farms in 2024, and 88 percent of them are classified as small farms. Small farming is not the exception. It is American agriculture. And high-value intensive production is how small farms stay solvent in a system that wasn't exactly built for them.

sections.heading: Why Herbs Are the Best Starting Point
sections.body:
I say this constantly, and I'll keep saying it until everyone believes me: if you are getting started in market gardening, grow herbs first.

Here's why. Herbs are only about growing leaves. That is the whole thing. You're not waiting for a fruit to set. You're not managing blossom pollination. You're not watching for that precise moment of ripeness. You cut leaves, you sell leaves, the plant pushes out more leaves. That's the cycle. It's the easiest possible entry point into intensive growing.

In my own garden right now I'm running beds of mint, oregano, rosemary, and parsley. Mint goes absolutely crazy in Houston. Fragrant, lush, aggressive — it will fill a bed if you let it. Oregano and rosemary are perennials, which means once they're established, they just keep giving. Parsley carpets out beautifully in South Texas conditions. I've grown it before and watched it spread and thicken and become one of the most productive beds in my garden.

The economics of herbs are almost too good to be true for a beginner. Texas A&M AgriLife Extension confirms that culinary herbs like cilantro, rosemary, basil, and parsley are well-suited to Texas growing conditions and represent some of the easiest-to-manage crops for both home and market production. Harris County AgriLife — that's right here in the Houston area — even publishes specific herb variety guides and organic vegetable production guides for our climate.

And here's the community angle that really gets me fired up. When someone at your farmers market booth buys a bunch of fresh rosemary, they go home and they cook with it. Then they come back next week because they used it all and it was so much better than the dried stuff in the plastic jar from the grocery store. That's your returning customer. That's your community relationship. Herbs build it fast.

One more thing. Incorporating fresh herbs into every meal — going to your little herb bed before dinner — is how people start building a food culture around locally grown produce. I've watched it happen. You give someone a bunch of fresh basil, you've changed the way they think about food. That's the goal, y'all. That's the whole point.

sections.heading: High-Value Crops for Small Spaces
sections.body:
Now let's talk about the crops that punch above their weight when space is tight. Because most of us market gardening in urban or suburban environments are not working hundreds of acres. We're working raised beds. We're working quarter-acres. We're working whatever we can get.

That's actually fine. That's better than fine. The Market Gardener — Jean-Martin Fortier's model — is built around the idea that intensive production on a small scale outperforms conventional large-scale production on a per-dollar-invested basis when you're selling direct. The crops that matter for this model are the ones with the highest return per bed-foot and the fastest turn times.

**Salad mix** is the workhorse of any serious market garden table. Baby greens — arugula, spinach, mizuna, red lettuce, mustard — cut young, packaged beautifully, and sold to customers who will pay a real premium for locally grown, freshly cut salad that doesn't have 1,500 food miles on it. At $20 per pound and a new harvest every three weeks from the same beds, the math works out quickly.

**Microgreens** are worth understanding even if they feel niche. Penn State Extension documents the business case in detail: startup costs are low, grow time is 7 to 14 days depending on variety, and wholesale prices to restaurants average $25 to $50 per pound. Radish, cilantro, and pea shoot microgreens rank among the highest profitability varieties because they balance fast growth, minimal inputs, and strong market demand. If you have a basement or a spare room with grow lights, microgreens are a year-round cash crop in any Texas climate — indoor or out.

**Radishes** deserve their own paragraph because they are criminally underrated. A 25-day crop that fits into any rotation gap. Succession-plant them every two weeks and you've always got a fresh batch for the table. Direct seeded, minimal fuss, and they sell. People love radishes at farmers markets, especially the colorful French breakfast and watermelon radish varieties that look different from the grocery store stuff.

The key principle across all three of these crops is the same: short turn times equal more cash flow cycles per year. Three lettuce harvests per season versus one tomato harvest — which one is actually more profitable? For small space intensive growers, it's usually the lettuce.

sections.heading: Adding Eggs and Ferments to Your Market Table
sections.body:
Here's where Scotty's particular story gets a little interesting, because my market table has never been just produce.

For five years, I was Scotty's Fermented Foods. Kimchi, escabeche, krauts, fermented vegetables — I was selling at Bay Area Farmers Market, Bay Brook, Urban Harvest, and even got my products into Whole Foods and Central Market for a time. I gave talks on fermented foods at the Houston Botanical Gardens. Fermentation was my entry point into the farmers market ecosystem.

What I learned from all those years is that value-added products are a completely different conversation at the market table. When you've got live kimchi sitting next to your fresh herbs, you're not just a grower anymore — you're a food producer. The customer relationship deepens. They're buying your food philosophy, not just your food.

Fermented foods at farmers markets fall under what the industry calls value-added products — and they are a legitimate and growing piece of the farmers market economy. The global fermented food market was valued at $126.5 billion in 2024 and is projected to nearly double by 2034, per Emergen Research market analysis. Consumer interest in gut health, local food, and live-culture foods is not a trend. It's a permanent shift.

Duck eggs are another layer to the operation. Ducks are part of a regenerative farm ecosystem — they eat pests, contribute fertility, and produce eggs that are richer, larger, and more complex in flavor than chicken eggs. At farmers markets, duck eggs command a real premium because most people have never tasted one. They're a conversation starter at your table. They tell a story about a diversified, living farm system. And in Texas, the licensing requirements for selling eggs at farmers markets are manageable — just make sure you understand your state agricultural compliance requirements before you start.

Diversification is how market gardeners survive the rough weeks. One crop fails? You've still got your eggs and your ferments. Weather wipes out your salad mix? Your herbs are still producing. The table stays interesting. The customers keep coming back.

sections.heading: What Scotty Grows at the Lab
sections.body:
Let me be specific about where I am right now, because I think there's value in watching someone build this thing in real time.

I'm starting my next chapter as a market gardener. I've been working on my regenerative garden in Houston for a couple of years. The whole philosophy is no synthetic fertilizers, no pesticides, nothing artificial — just well-made humus and living soil doing what it's supposed to do. Nutrient-dense food grown in biology-rich ground.

Right now my beds have perennial herbs as the foundation: mint, oregano, rosemary, parsley. These are the backbone of my market table going forward. They're low-maintenance, perennial producers that fill space and generate consistent weekly income.

I'm growing as a rotational gardener on a small footprint. My beds cycle through different crops to maintain soil health, prevent pest buildup, and keep the market table changing with the seasons. In Texas, that seasonal calendar is long — we get spring crops, fall crops, and winter production that most northern growers can't pull off.

I'm also starting a community garden project in Spring Branch and the Needville area. This is the piece I'm most excited about. Because what I really want is not just to grow food — I want to build a local food movement. I want to show people in my neighborhood that this is possible. That you can grow food to sell. That you can generate income from a small plot. That farmers markets don't have to be filled with middlemen buying wholesale produce and marking it up.

I've seen that at the markets, y'all. Produce that looks perfect and uniform because it came from a produce distributor, not a garden. When you grow it yourself, the vegetables look different — irregular, colorful, real. That's how you know it's the genuine article.

My goal is guerrilla gardens in every dead space in this city. Gardeners selling real food at every market. A community where local food sovereignty isn't just an idea — it's Tuesday morning at the farmers market.

sections.heading: Getting Started as a Market Gardener
sections.body:
Alright, let's bring this all the way back to practical. You want to start. Here's what I actually recommend.

**Start with one herb bed.** Not ten beds, not a greenhouse full of microgreens, not a full rotation plan. One bed of perennial herbs. Mint and basil if you're in Texas — they thrive in our climate and they sell at every single market. ATTRA's Market Gardening: A Start Up Guide (available free at attra.ncat.org) is the best single resource I know for the business side of this. Read it before you spend a dollar on infrastructure.

**Know your market before you plant.** Visit your local farmers market multiple times before you commit to what you're growing. What's already there in abundance? What's missing? In Houston, the Bay Area Farmers Market and Urban Harvest markets have strong vendor bases. Find the gap. Fill it. Don't grow what six other people are already growing — grow what the market needs.

**Add one fast crop per season.** Once your herbs are established, layer in one high-turn crop — salad mix or radishes are my first recommendations. These give you cash flow while your perennial herbs mature and multiply. They teach you bed management, succession planting, and harvest timing without overwhelming you.

**Track your numbers.** ATTRA and SARE both emphasize record-keeping as one of the biggest differentiators between market gardens that stay profitable and those that don't. Know your cost per bed per crop, your revenue per market, your return per hour of labor. This is a business. Treat it like one.

**Connect with your community.** The farmers market is not just a transaction point. It's a relationship-building platform. Talk to your customers. Learn what they cook. Tell them how you grew it. A loyal customer who comes back every week is worth ten first-time buyers. Build your community around real food, and the market will take care of itself.

I believe with everything I've got that local food systems are how we take back some control over what goes into our bodies and our communities. Market gardening is a piece of that. Regenerative practices are a piece of that. Fermented foods are a piece of that. Duck eggs are a piece of that.

Grow something real. Sell it direct. Feed your neighbors.

That's the whole thing, y'all.

sources.title: Market Gardening: A Start Up Guide

sources.note: Comprehensive free guide covering crop selection, business planning, and market strategy for beginning market gardeners
